subj-group.subject: Article(s) from the Special Issue on Null findings in basic, cognitive and clinical neuroscience studies; Edited by Dr. Gesa Hartwigsen

title-group.article-title: No associations in preregistered study of youth depression and functional connectivity of fronto-parietal and default mode networks

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Psychological & Brain Sciences, Washington University in St. Louis, One Brookings Drive, St. Louis, MO, 63105, USA
bDepartment of Psychiatry, Washington University School of Medicine, 660 S. Euclid Avenue, St. Louis, MO, 63110, USA
cDepartment of Radiology, Washington University School of Medicine, 660 S. Euclid Avenue, St. Louis, MO, 63110, USA

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Adolescence is characterized by vulnerability to the onset of major depressive disorder (MDD). The goal of this preregistered study was to assess neural correlates of depression symptoms in young adolescents, both cross-sectionally and longitudinally. The default mode network (DMN) is believed to support internal attention towards self-referential thoughts, while the fronto-parietal network (FPN) is theorized to support cognitive control and regulation of attention. As MDD diagnosis has been associated with heightened connectivity within DMN regions and diminished connectivity within FPN regions relative to healthy controls, our study builds upon group-difference analyses by using dimensional measures of depression severity. Our preregistered hypotheses were that within-DMN functional connectivity would be positively associated with concurrent depression severity, while within-FPN functional connectivity would be negatively associated with concurrent depression severity. Preregistered analyses also examined between DMN-FPN connectivity as an alternative predictor variable, and assessed the longitudinal associations between all three functional connectivity measures and change in depression severity over three subsequent waves. Multiple regression models tested cross-sectional analyses and hierarchical linear models tested longitudinal analyses. One hundred and twenty-four youth completed a resting state functional MRI. Their depression severity was assessed at the time of the scan and at three follow-up sessions. None of the predictor variables were associated with concurrent depression severity, nor with the slope of depression symptom trajectories in longitudinal analyses. These negative results add to extant cross-sectional studies, and may inform future investigations of brain correlates of depression psychopathology in youth.

list-item.p: Preregistered study of functional connectivity (FC) and depression in 124 youth.
Within-network FC of DMN and FPN unrelated to concurrent depression.
Between-network FC of DMN with FPN unrelated to concurrent depression.
FC of DMN and FPN unrelated to future depression trajectories.

body.p: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), characterized by a loss of interest and persistent low mood, is the leading cause of disability worldwide (World Health Organization, 2020) and contributes greatly to global disease burden (Murray et al., 2012). Around 2% of pre-pubertal children will receive a diagnosis of MDD, with that number rising to 7% or even 13% in adolescence (Perou et al., 2013; Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration, 2017). Youth depression predicts earlier recurrence and higher risk for other mood disorders (Kovacs, 1996), as well as a host of adverse socio-economic outcomes, including lower educational attainment (Clayborne et al., 2019), increased work impairment (Keenan-Miller et al., 2007), and poorer mental health outcomes in adulthood (Fergusson and Woodward, 2002; D. Johnson et al., 2018). Most alarmingly, youth depression is a major risk factor for suicide (Kandel et al., 1991; Perou et al., 2013)—the third leading cause of death for 5 to 14-year-olds (Kochanek et al., 2019). Therefore, youth depression is a serious health problem which requires extensive research efforts and immediate intervention.

Recent research initiatives, such as the Research Domain Criteria (RDoC), have proposed that a mechanistic understanding of psychiatric disorders will lead to more accurate diagnoses and effective treatments (Insel et al., 2010). One proposed mechanism is that large-scale brain networks, when dysregulated, disrupt typical functioning and contribute to psychiatric disorders such as MDD (Menon, 2011). Resting-state functional connectivity MRI (rs-fcMRI) measures correlations of spontaneous low-frequency fluctuation in blood oxygen level dependent (BOLD) signals among brain regions in subjects at rest (Biswal et al., 1995). This allows neuroscientists to explore functional network structure of the brain (Bullmore and Sporns, 2009). It can be used to understand differences in network connectivity in clinical populations independent of any task paradigm, thereby avoiding confounders associated with task implementation (Fox and Greicius, 2010).

Previous rs-fcMRI studies have associated hyperconnectivity within the default mode network (DMN) regions with MDD diagnosis (Alexopoulos et al., 2012, Greicius et al., 2007, Jin et al., 2011, Kaiser et al., 2015, Rzepa and McCabe, 2018, Zhao et al., 2019, Zhu et al., 2012, Zhu et al., 2017). The DMN, initially defined using rs-fcMRI (Greicius et al., 2003), is posited to support a “task-negative mode” (Greicius et al., 2003; Raichle et al., 2001) and self-referential processes (Gusnard et al., 2001; Northoff et al., 2006; Sheline et al., 2009). Its key nodes include the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC), the precuneus, and the posterior cingulate cortex (PCC), all of which are believed to support self-related consciousness and mental representations (Cavanna and Trimble, 2006; Meyer and Lieberman, 2018; Johnson et al., 2002). Rumination, a form of excessive, repetitive thinking about one's feelings and problems without actively addressing these problems (Nolen-Hoeksema et al., 2008), is a maladaptive type of self-referential process. Rumination has been associated with increased negative affect (Mor and Winquist, 2002) and depressive symptoms (Sarin et al., 2005; Segerstrom et al., 2000; Spasojević and Alloy, 2001). Therefore, it has been proposed that MDD patients exhibit hyperconnected DMN which underlies their excessive rumination (Alexopoulos et al., 2012, Berman et al., 2011), leaving insufficient attention and cognitive resources for external salient stimuli.

Hypoconnectivity within the frontoparietal network (FPN), which is believed to support top-down executive control (Dosenbach et al., 2007; Seeley et al., 2007; Vincent et al., 2008), has also been associated with MDD diagnosis in several rs-fcMRI studies (Alexopoulos et al., 2012, Buchanan et al., 2014, Kaiser et al., 2015, Li et al., 2015, Pan et al., 2020, Wu et al., 2017). The network structure of the FPN was first defined using task fcMRI (Dosenbach et al., 2007). Key nodes of the FPN have been linked to major cognitive control processes which may be disrupted in depression. As MDD is characterized by moderate deficits across multiple cognitive domains (Millan et al., 2012; Rock et al., 2013; Zuckerman et al., 2018), within-FPN hypoconnectivity may contribute to cognitive dysfunctions in MDD (Cole et al., 2014).

Reduced connectivity between the FPN and DMN has also been highlighted by some previous rs-fcMRI studies in MDD patients (Kaiser et al., 2015) and in subclinical depression (Hwang et al., 2015) relative to healthy controls, and was associated with rumination (Connolly et al., 2013; Lydon-Staley et al., 2019) and other depressive symptoms (Hwang et al., 2015). Since the FPN is densely connected with many other large-scale networks (Marek et al., 2015; Power et al., 2013), its connections with attention networks such as the dorsal attention network and the DMN enable it to regulate the balance between external and internal attention resources (Dixon et al., 2018; Vincent et al., 2008). Therefore, reduced connectivity between the FPN and the DMN may reflect weak top-down FPN regulation of internally-oriented attention, leading to depressive symptoms such as excessive attention diverted to internal thoughts as opposed to the external environment (Kaiser et al., 2015; Marchetti et al., 2012; Nejad et al., 2013).

In summary, previous rs-fcMRI research has provided empirical support for heightened within-DMN connectivity, diminished within-FPN connectivity, and diminished connectivity between DMN and FPN as neural correlates of MDD. A previous study found hyperconnectivity between posterior cingulate seed and medial prefrontal regions in children with a history of MDD diagnosis during preschool age relative to healthy controls, and reduced connectivity between the posterior cingulate seed and lateral cortical regions (Gaffrey et al., 2012). However, most prior studies with adolescents have focused on cross-sectional associations, with only a few longitudinal studies conducted (Connolly et al., 2013; Davey et al., 2015; Ellis et al., 2017; Hirshfeld-Becker et al., 2019; P.M. Pan et al., 2017; Strikwerda-Brown et al., 2014). Furthermore, research studying links between depression and functional connectivity prior to adolescence has been lacking (Luby et al., 2009). As a result, it is still unclear if neural correlates of childhood depression differ from those of adolescent and adult depression (Tang et al., 2018). Preschool-onset depression has been found to be a strong predictor of developing MDD in later childhood (Luby et al., 2014) and thus requires further research and clinical attention.

The aim of this study was to test associations between a priori functional connectivity (i.e. within-DMN, within-FPN and between FPN-DMN connectivity) and youth depression both cross-sectionally and prospectively. A sample from the Preschool Depression Study (PDS) was used to test the preregistered hypotheses that concurrent depression severity, assessed as the mean of parental and child reports, is positively associated with functional connectivity during rest within the DMN and negatively associated with functional connectivity during rest within the FPN. These associations, if significant, would lend support to the theory that adolescent depression results from excessive internal attention to self-referencing thoughts due to deficient frontal control. Additional preregistered analyses were conducted on the cross-sectional relationship between concurrent depression severity and inter-network functional connectivity. Longitudinal analyses tested whether any of the three functional connectivity metrics (i.e., within-DMN, within-FPN, and inter-network DMN-FPN) predicted change in concurrent depression severity over three subsequent assessments. On top of the preregistered analyses, follow-up analyses which were not preregistered assessed the same associations using a) concurrent depression severity reported separately by parents and children and b) past depression severity (i.e. period from the beginning of the study when participants were aged between 3 and 6 until the time of scan) as an alternative measure of depression severity.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Two hundred and ten 6 to 12-year-olds who had been followed longitudinally since preschool-age. In this longitudinal study, 3- to 6-year-old children were recruited from primary care practices and preschools/daycares throughout the St. Louis metropolitan region using a validated screening checklist (Preschool Feelings Checklist [PFC]; Luby et al., 2004) to oversample preschoolers with symptoms of depression, externalizing disorders, and healthy controls. Children were excluded if they presented with chronic illness, marked speech and/or language delays and/or neurologic or autism spectrum disorders. An additional 42 healthy controls (i.e. without current psychiatric disorders nor prior depressive episodes) were added at school age and were eligible for the fMRI assessment; of note, these subjects did not have a measure of past depression severity. Of these subjects, 124 participants completed the fMRI and had useable functional connectivity data. Of those, 116 had available child-reported concurrent depression data, and 117 had available parent-reported concurrent depression.

Mahalanobis distance was calculated for each multivariate relationship to be examined (e.g., within-network functional connectivity of the DMN and concurrent depression severity) and used to identify and exclude multivariate outliers with a threshold of p < .001 (N = 3 for mean parent and child reported concurrent depression and within-network functional connectivity of the DMN or FPN; N = 2 for mean parent and child reported concurrent depression and between DMN-FPN functional connectivity; N = 2 for child-reported concurrent depression and within-network functional connectivity of the DMN or FPN; N = 1 for parent-reported concurrent depression and both within-network functional connectivity of the DMN or FPN and between DMN-FPN connectivity; N = 1 for child-reported concurrent depression and between DMN-FPN connectivity). Detailed demographic information can be found in Table 1. Parental written consent and child assent were obtained before participation and the local Institutional Review Board approved all procedures.Table 1
Sample Characteristics and demographics for cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses.Table 1

MDD = Major Depressive Disorder. SD = standard deviation.

sec.title: Measure of depression severity
sec.p: There were two measures of depression severity used in the current study. Raw total scores from the Children's Depression Inventory (CDI) (Kovacs, 1985) were used to measure concurrent and longitudinal depression severity. CDI scores from three subsequent waves were used for longitudinal analyses. The mean of child and parent report on the CDI was calculated for each assessment wave. Of the 124 participants included in the longitudinal analyses, 119, 106, 94 and 94 had available CDI scores in the first to the fourth assessments, respectively.

Because the CDI scores had not been collected prior to the scan, mean number of core symptoms of depression endorsed on a semi-structured interview from the beginning of the PDS study (i.e., at preschool age) until before the time of scan was used to measure past depression severity (N = 96). For all measures of depression, higher scores indicate greater depression severity.

sec.title: Covariates
sec.p: The following covariates were dummy coded: child's race (dummy coded using two variables to account for biracial participants: White: Yes = 1, No = 0; Black: Yes = 1, No = 0), sex (male = 1, female = 0), MDD history (present = 1, absent = 0). MDD history here refers to whether participants had received a diagnosis of MDD from the beginning of the study until the time of scan. The following continuous covariates were included: child's age at scan, income-to-needs ratio, concurrent anxiety severity at time of scan. The income-to-needs ratio was measured as the total family income divided by the federal poverty level based on family size (McLoyd, 1998) at time of scan. Concurrent anxiety severity was calculated as the number of core symptoms of generalized, social, and separation anxiety disorders, as well as post-traumatic stress disorder reported on the Child and Adolescent Psychiatric Assessment (CAPA; Angold and Costello, 1995; Angold et al., 1995) at the time of scan.

Analyses examining associations between functional connectivity and past depression severity covaried for past anxiety severity, which was calculated as the mean number of anxiety symptoms endorsed (on the Preschool Age Psychiatric Assessment [PAPA; Egger and Angold, 2004; Egger et al., 2006] when children were less than 8-years-old, and on the CAPA when youth were 8-years-old or older) from the start of the PDS study (i.e., at preschool age) up until before the time of scan.

sec.title: Data acquisition
sec.p: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data were acquired using a 3.0 T Siemens Prisma scanner. Resting-state functional MRI (rsfMRI) was acquired with an echoplanar imaging sequence (TR = 720 ms, TE 33 ms, FOV = 216 mm, flip = 52°, voxel size 2.4 × 2.4 x 2.4, 63 slices, Multiband = 7). A T1-weighted anatomical image was acquired for spatial normalization and functional localization (TR = 2400 ms, TE 2.22 ms, flip angle = 8°, 208 slices, FOV = 240 mm, voxel size 0.8 × 0.8 x 0.8).

sec.title: Functional connectivity preprocessing
sec.p: A combination of FSL and in-house MATLAB scripts were used for functional connectivity data preprocessing and analysis. EPI images were corrected for gradient distortions, readout distortions, and bias field using fieldmap correction. Images were realigned from multi-band functional frames to a single-band reference image, and resampled to 2 mm isotropic and registered to the MNI152 atlas. High motion volumes were censored (0.2 mm frame displacement). Respiratory artifacts in head motion estimates were corrected using a 2nd order bandstop filter (0.31 Hz and 0.43 Hz). Volumes were demeaned and de-trended using betas from low-motion frames. Signals of non-interest, including 24 motion regressors (6 head motion parameters, their derivatives and squares), the first five PCA components from white matter and CSF, whole brain global signal, and the global signal derivative, were entered as regressors, with betas computed from low-motion frames. Data were interpolated across censored frames and bandpass filtered at 0.009 Hz < f < 0.08 Hz. Finally, data were smoothed using 4 mm FWHM Gaussian kernel. Participants with at least 5 min of low-motion fcMRI data were included in analyses.

sec.title: Extracting estimates of functional connectivity
sec.p: Functional connectivity during rest was measured as the temporal correlation of BOLD signal activities within the FPN network and DMN network separately, as well as the correlation between the FPN and DMN networks. Networks were defined by the parcellation from Power et al. (2011). Pearson r correlations were calculated between mean timeseries for all voxels in each ROI forming subject-specific connectomes. “Within” network connectivity was assessed by computing pair-wise correlations between all pairs of ROIs within a network (e.g., all ROIs in the FPN) and averaging across them to generate a single value. “Between” network connectivity was assessed by computing the pair-wise correlations of each ROI in one network (e.g., FPN) to each ROI in another network (e.g., DMN) and averaging across them to generate a single value.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p:
Confirmatory and follow-up analyses and hypotheses are specified, with confirmatory analyses and hypotheses preregistered post data collection but prior to data analysis (osf.io/zjpqu). Confirmatory analyses tested the preregistered hypotheses that concurrent depression severity, assessed as the mean of parental and child reports, would be positively associated with functional connectivity during rest within the DMN and negatively associated with functional connectivity during rest within the FPN. These hypotheses were tested using multiple linear regression models. Other preregistered analyses without specific hypotheses included testing the association between inter-network (i.e. between DMN-FPN) functional connectivity and concurrent depression severity using multiple regression models. Longitudinal analyses also tested the relationship between baseline within-network and inter-network functional connectivity metrics and individual trajectories of depression severity using hierarchical linear modeling. Linear mixed effect models from the lme4 package in Rv3.6.0 (Bates et al., 2015) were used to model fixed and random effects of intercept and slope parameters over four assessment waves, with restricted maximum likelihood estimation (REML) used to estimate parameters. Supplementary analyses fit latent growth curve models with lavaan (Rosseel, 2012) using full information maximum likelihood to account for missing data and maximum likelihood estimation with robust (Huber-White) standard errors to estimate parameters. These models were used to test for associations between within-network and inter-network connectivity at time of scan and the slope of depression severity trajectories over the following three waves. In addition to the aforementioned covariates, psychotropic medication use within 48 h of the time of scan was included as an additional covariate in supplementary analyses. The same set of cross-sectional and longitudinal associations were re-analyzed in follow-up analyses using alternative outcome variables (i.e. past depression severity, parent-reported concurrent depression severity, and child-reported concurrent depression severity). Analyses controlled for age at time of scan, sex, race, income-to-needs ratio, MDD history (i.e. lifetime diagnosis of depression prior to time of scan), and concurrent anxiety severity. Analyses of past depression severity excluded MDD history and included past anxiety severity as a covariate. Continuous predictors and outcome variables were standardized to make multiple regression estimates more interpretable; however, measures were unstandardized for hierarchical linear models and latent growth curve models. False discovery rate (FDR) multiple comparison correction was applied across multiple regression models (see Table 3). See Table 2 for bivariate correlations between all continuous variables.Table 2
Bivariate correlations between continuous variables in main analyses.Table 2

All estimates are standardized. DMN = default mode network. FPN = fronto-parietal network.

sec.title: Association between functional connectivity and concurrent depression severity
sec.p: Neither within-DMN nor within-FPN functional connectivity were associated with concurrent depression severity at the time of scan (see Table 3). Inter-network functional connectivity between the DMN and the FPN was also not significantly associated with concurrent depression severity. All findings were consistent when child and parent reported concurrent depression severity were examined separately, when anxiety symptom severity was excluded as a covariate (see Supplemental Materials Table S1), and when psychotropic medication use was included as a covariate (see Supplemental Materials Table S2). When the same analyses were rerun examining past depression severity, findings were consistent. While we did find a nominally significant association between past depression severity and functional connectivity between the DMN and FPN, this association did not survive FDR correction, thus showing no significant associations with functional connectivity metrics (see Table 3).

sec.title: Association between functional connectivity and depression severity trajectories
sec.p:
No function connectivity metrics of interest (i.e. within-DMN, within-FPN and between DMN-FPN functional connectivity metrics) were significantly associated with the slope of depression symptom trajectories (see Table 4). Findings also remained consistent when latent growth curve models using maximum likelihood estimation with robust (Huber-White) standard errors (see Supplemental Materials Table S5) were conducted to supplement hierarchical linear models. All findings were consistent when child and parent reported depression severity were examined separately (see Table 4), when anxiety symptom severity was excluded as a covariate (see Supplemental Materials Table S3), and when psychotropic medication use was included as a covariate (see Supplemental Materials Table S4).Table 4
Results of hierarchical linear models of associations between depression severity trajectories and within-DMN, within-FPN and between DMN-FPN functional connectivity.Table 4

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The current study did not find support for the pre-registered hypotheses. The results of follow-up analyses were also null. Specifically, within-DMN functional connectivity, within-FPN functional connectivity, and between DMN-FPN functional connectivity were not significantly associated with concurrent or past depression severity, nor predicted future change in individual depression severity trajectories.

Although we are cautious in drawing conclusions from null results, these results provide important caution for propositions that DMN, FPN, or between network functional connectivity during rest is a neural correlate of depression or depression risk in childhood. This is key information in evaluating support for theories that suggest that weak regulation from cognitive control networks to the extended medial prefrontal DMN network may underlie deficits in regulating excessive internally-oriented attention in depression. Such theories propose that imbalanced connectivity between the DMN and the FPN, coupled with a hypo-connected FPN and hyper-connected DMN, may underlie ineffective top-down regulation of negative emotions and attention, resulting in persistent negative mood and cognitive bias, dysfunctions across cognitive domains, as well as excessive internally-oriented attention at the expense of attending to the external world in depressed patients (Brzezicka, 2013; Kaiser et al., 2015; B. J. Li et al., 2017; Marchetti et al., 2012; Nejad et al., 2013).

The current study, then, may provide some insight into boundaries of this theory. For example, this theory was developed in large part using data from adult participants. Clinically, depression in youth differs from adult manifestations of depression in important ways, such as greater irritability (Stringaris et al., 2013) which is classified as a core mood symptom for children and adolescents in the DSM-IV and DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association, 2000, 2013). Neurally, basic science research shows that the DMN is sparsely functionally correlated during childhood, while it has developed into a tightly integrated network by the age of 21 (Fair et al., 2008), and that the FPN continues to change during preadolescence (Sherman et al., 2014). Therefore, ongoing network development may lead to key differences in the neural correlates of childhood and adult depression. Cognitively, children may be continuing to develop cognitive control abilities and acquire more complex cognitive emotion regulation abilities into adolescence and adulthood (Larsen and Luna, 2018; Luna et al., 2015). Therefore, there are many reasons to believe that some of the brain correlates of depression in youth may differ from those in adult. This question warrants future studies, in particular large-scale longitudinal studies such as the currently ongoing Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study.

Although the current study failed to reject the null hypothesis, it had notable methodological strengths. It used a unique, longitudinal sample of youth enhanced for early onset depression and prospectively assessed for depression severity across four waves. It accounted for covariates commonly associated with youth depression, such as socioeconomic status (Goodman et al., 2003; Pino et al., 2018). Furthermore, it was preregistered. There are inherent values in the publication of preregistered null results. Publication bias, which refers to the phenomenon that studies with negative results are less often published than those with positive results, has increased, especially in the field of social science (Fanelli, 2012; Nosek et al., 2018). Publication bias may encourage researchers to withhold their negative research findings, or even engage in questionable research practices such as p-hacking and HARKing (Hypothesizing After the Results are Known) (Head et al., 2015; John et al., 2012; Kerr, 1998). These practices may lead to increased false positive effects (Simmons et al., 2011) and poor replicability (Open Science Collaboration, 2015). Publication bias is also detrimental to the development of knowledge, because hypotheses of new studies are crafted based on previous literature. If there is an inflated ratio of positive findings relative to negative findings, researchers may be misguided to believe that there is a robust relationship when there is not, and predicate their hypotheses on questionable theories (Joober et al., 2012). In order to check the questionable research practices, preregistration has been proposed as a way to distinguish prediction and postdiction, and to restrict unwanted researcher's degree of freedom which can manipulate results (Silberzahn et al., 2018). Some studies have suggested that preregistration is associated with increased reproducibility (Swaen et al., 2001) and less publication bias (Kaplan and Irvin, 2015). Therefore, despite reporting null results, our preregistered study makes a valuable contribution to the literature.

There are also potential methodological reasons for why the current study did not mirror prior findings. Firstly, it is possible that this study failed to detect a true effect due to insufficient power. We conducted power analyses (WebPower package) showing that the current study had 80% power to detect effect sizes (f2) larger than 0.074–0.09, depending on the analysis. Secondly, most previous studies were conducted with youth over the age of 10, while about half of the participants in his study ranged from 6 to 9 years old. This may explain the divergence in the current study's findings from previous findings using adult samples. It is possible that as the brain develops, resting-state functional connectivity metrics become more associated with depression over the course of adolescence and into adulthood, but are not yet associated in childhood (i.e. below the age of twelve). Since we were limited to one wave of functional connectivity in the current study when participants were about 10 years old, we were unable to directly test this hypothesis. However, it is an interesting and important avenue for future studies. Thirdly, some studies have identified hyperconnectivity among some regions and hypoconnectivity among other regions typically included in the DMN (Connolly et al., 2013; Gaffrey et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). There is evidence of functional heterogeneity within the FPN (Dixon et al., 2018) and the DMN (Andrews-Hanna et al., 2010; Laird et al., 2009; Leech et al., 2011; Sambataro et al., 2014). In contrast, the current study computed DMN and FPN functional connectivity using all regions included in these networks. As such, it is possible that these opposing effects cancelled out in the current study. Finally, most previous studies used categorical measures of depression (e.g., comparing MDD and healthy controls) and ANOVA/ANCOVAs, while the present study used dimensional measures of depression and multiple regression analyses.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In conclusion, the present, preregistered study did not find significant cross-sectional or longitudinal associations between depression severity and intra-network functional connectivity within the DMN or FPN networks, nor between the two networks. Future studies will further inform the presence or potential absence of any relationship between functional connectivity and depression in youth.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This work was supported by National Institute of Mental Health grants 2R01 MH064769-06 and R01 MH098454.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Yueyue Qu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Brent I. Rappaport: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Joan L. Luby: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Deanna M. Barch: Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Dr. Joan Luby receives royalties from Guildford Press. No other authors report disclosures.
